source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure the GKE Metadata Server is Enabled

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
input_row.title: Ensure the GKE Metadata Server is Enabled
input_row.description: Running the GKE Metadata Server prevents workloads from accessing sensitive instance metadata and facilitates Workload Identity.
input_row.rationale: Every node stores its metadata on a metadata server. Some of this metadata, such as kubelet credentials and the VM instance identity token, is sensitive and should not be exposed to a Kubernetes workload. Enabling the GKE Metadata server prevents pods (that are not running on the host network) from accessing this metadata and facilitates Workload Identity. When unspecified, the default setting allows running pods to have full access to the node's underlying metadata server. Impact: The GKE Metadata Server must be run when using Workload Identity. Because Workload Identity replaces the need to use Metadata Concealment, the two approaches are incompatible. When the GKE Metadata Server and Workload Identity are enabled, unless the Pod is running on the host network, Pods cannot use the the Compute Engine default service account. Workloads may need modification in order for them to use Workload Identity as described within: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload- identity.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. From the list of clusters, click on the name of the cluster of interest and for each Node pool within the cluster, open the Details pane, and ensure that the GKE Metadata Server is set to Enabled. Using Command Line To check whether the GKE Metadata Server is enabled for each Node pool within a cluster, run the following command: gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <cluster_zone> -- format json | jq .nodePools[].config.workloadMetadataConfig This should return the following for each Node pool: { "mode": "GKE_METADATA" } Null ({ }) is returned if the GKE Metadata Server is not enabled.
input_row.remediation: The GKE Metadata Server requires Workload Identity to be enabled on a cluster. Modify the cluster to enable Workload Identity and enable the GKE Metadata Server. Using Google Cloud Console 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. From the list of clusters, select the cluster for which Workload Identity is disabled. 3. Under the DETAILS pane, navigate down to the Security subsection. 4. Click on the pencil icon named Edit Workload Identity, click on Enable Workload Identity in the pop-up window, and select a workload pool from the drop-down box. By default, it will be the namespace of the Cloud project containing the cluster, for example: <project_id>.svc.id.goog. 5. Click SAVE CHANGES and wait for the cluster to update. 6. Once the cluster has updated, select each Node pool within the cluster Details page. 7. For each Node pool, select EDIT within the Node pool details page. 8. Within the Edit node pool pane, check the Enable GKE Metadata Server checkbox. 9. Click SAVE. Using Command Line gcloud container clusters update <cluster_name> --identity- namespace=<project_id>.svc.id.goog Note that existing Node pools are unaffected. New Node pools default to --workload- metadata-from-node=GKE_METADATA_SERVER. To modify an existing Node pool to enable GKE Metadata Server: gcloud container node-pools update <node_pool_name> --cluster=<cluster_name> --workload-metadata-from-node=GKE_METADATA_SERVER Workloads may need modification in order for them to use Workload Identity as described within: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload- identity. Default Value: By default, running pods to have full access to the node's underlying metadata server. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/protecting-cluster- metadata#concealment 2. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload-identity 3. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/workload-identity
input_row.impact: The GKE Metadata Server must be run when using Workload Identity. Because Workload Identity replaces the need to use Metadata Concealment, the two approaches are incompatible. When the GKE Metadata Server and Workload Identity are enabled, unless the Pod is running on the host network, Pods cannot use the the Compute Engine default service account. Workloads may need modification in order for them to use Workload Identity as described within: https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/workload- identity.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

- The control can be verified via GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud).
- The audit field provides a specific `gcloud` command to verify the configuration: 
  ```bash
  gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <cluster_zone> --format json | jq .nodePools[].config.workloadMetadataConfig
  ```
- This is a standard configuration property for GKE clusters and node pools, making it suitable for automated checks.
- The described process does not require SSH access to instances or other manual verification steps.
- The check can be executed programmatically, following the standard automation practices for CSPM tools.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_node_pool_gke_metadata_server_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **GCP CLI Commands:** Use the `gcloud container clusters describe` command to inspect the configuration of GKE clusters and their node pools.
- **Configuration Property:** Look for `workloadMetadataConfig` in the node pool configuration and ensure it has the `mode` set to `GKE_METADATA`.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:** 
  - **Pass:** The `mode` of `workloadMetadataConfig` is set to `GKE_METADATA` for every node pool.
  - **Fail:** The `mode` is not set to `GKE_METADATA`, or the `workloadMetadataConfig` property is null.
- **Resources to Enumerate:** All GKE clusters and their respective node pools must be checked within the organization/project.
- Enumerate through each cluster by executing `gcloud container clusters list` to retrieve cluster names and zones, then apply the verification command to each.
- **Expected Secure Configuration:** All node pools should have the GKE Metadata Server enabled as indicated by the presence of `"mode": "GKE_METADATA"` in their workload metadata configuration.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
step2_review:
---

### 1. **APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated:** The classification as "Automated" is correct. The control can be programmatically checked using GCP APIs and the `gcloud` CLI.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI:** The `gcloud container clusters describe` command exposes the `workloadMetadataConfig` property for each node pool, which is the authoritative source for this configuration. This is accessible via both CLI and the GCP REST API (`projects.locations.clusters.nodePools.get`).
- **Other Automation Options:** While CloudFormation is AWS-specific, GCP alternatives like Deployment Manager or Terraform are not directly relevant for audit checks. GCP Config (now called Config Controller) does not natively provide compliance checks for this property, so the CLI/API approach is correct.

---

### 2. **TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** **Score: 9/10**

- **Automation Details:** The described method is technically correct. Enumerating clusters and node pools, then checking `workloadMetadataConfig.mode` is the industry standard approach.
- **gcloud CLI:** The command is valid. However, using `jq` is external to `gcloud` and assumes the environment has it installed. For pure GCP environments, consider using `gcloud`'s `--format` flag for JSONPath or custom output.
- **Manual Steps:** Not applicable; this is a fully automatable check.
- **Program Name:** `gcp_gke_node_pool_gke_metadata_server_enabled` is clear, but could be slightly more descriptive (see Improvements).

---

### 3. **IMPROVEMENTS:**

**a. Program Name:**
- **Suggestion:** `gcp_gke_node_pool_workload_metadata_mode_gke_metadata`
  - This is more precise, as "GKE Metadata Server" is enabled by setting `workloadMetadataConfig.mode` to `GKE_METADATA`. This avoids ambiguity if Google adds more modes in the future.

**b. Automation Details:**
- **Alternative API:** The same check can be performed via the GCP REST API:
  - `GET https://container.googleapis.com/v1/projects/{project}/locations/{location}/clusters/{cluster}/nodePools`
  - This is useful for organizations building custom CSPM tools or using service accounts without `gcloud`.
- **gcloud Output:** Prefer using `gcloud`'s built-in formatting:
  ```bash
  gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <zone> \
    --format="json(nodePools[].config.workloadMetadataConfig)"
  ```
  Or, for a table:
  ```bash
  gcloud container node-pools list --cluster=<cluster_name> --zone=<zone> \
    --format="table(name,config.workloadMetadataConfig.mode)"
  ```
- **Edge Cases:** Consider clusters with no node pools or node pools without `workloadMetadataConfig` (legacy clusters). Document how these are handled (fail/pass/skip).
- **Multi-Region:** Ensure the script iterates over all regions/zones, not just the default.

**c. Missing Considerations:**
- **Workload Identity:** The control is related to Workload Identity. If the organization uses GKE Autopilot, the metadata server is always enabled—document this.
- **Organization-wide Coverage:** Ensure all projects are enumerated, not just the current project.

---

### 4. **CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is technically sound, aligns with GCP best practices, and leverages the correct API/CLI methods.

---

### 5. **FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** **Modify**

- **Keep the automated classification.**
- **Modify the automation details** to:
  - Prefer `gcloud`'s built-in formatting over `jq` for portability.
  - Clarify handling of clusters/node pools with missing `workloadMetadataConfig`.
  - Suggest a more descriptive program name: `gcp_gke_node_pool_workload_metadata_mode_gke_metadata`.
  - Document that this check applies to both Standard and Autopilot clusters, noting that Autopilot always enables the metadata server.

---

## **Summary Table**

| Section             | Assessment                                                                                     |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation | AGREE                                                                                         |
| Technical Accuracy  | 9/10                                                                                          |
| Improvements        | Use gcloud formatting, clarify edge cases, improve program name, note Autopilot behavior      |
| Confidence          | HIGH                                                                                          |
| Final Recommendation| Modify: update automation details and naming, clarify edge cases, retain automated classification |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that the GKE Metadata Server setting is fully exposed via the GCP API and can be programmatically checked using the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`) or REST API. The `workloadMetadataConfig.mode` property for each node pool is directly accessible and unambiguous, allowing for reliable, repeatable automation without manual intervention or console inspection.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_gke_node_pool_gke_metadata_server_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Node Pools
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - Google Cloud SDK:  
    `gcloud container clusters describe <cluster_name> --zone <zone> --format="json"`
  - Or REST API:  
    `projects.locations.clusters.nodePools.get`
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - For each node pool: `.nodePools[].config.workloadMetadataConfig.mode`
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - The value of `workloadMetadataConfig.mode` is exactly `GKE_METADATA` for every node pool in every cluster.
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - Any node pool where `workloadMetadataConfig.mode` is not set to `GKE_METADATA` (e.g., `GCE_METADATA` or null/missing).

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There were no substantive disagreements between the AI opinions; both agreed on automation. The only minor suggestion was to ensure the program name is descriptive, which is satisfied by the provided name.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
